Query: All of the following are seen in MEN-1 syndrome except:

Options:
A: Posterior pituitary tumors
B: Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors
C: Foregut carcinoid
D: Parathyroid hyperplasia
Retrieved Documents:
Document [0] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Epidemiology) Diagnosis of MEN1 is established if the index case has at least two of the primary manifestations of MEN1 Syndrome (tumor of parathyroid glands, anterior pituitary gland, and a neuroendocrine component of gastro-entero-pancreatic tissue) or at least one MEN1-related tumor and a first-degree relative with confirmed MEN1 (positive MEN1 gene mutation), or finally, the index case has the pathologic mutation in the MEN1 gene.
Document [1] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Introduction) Multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN) constitutes a group of autosomal dominant disorders characterized by a broad spectrum of endocrine and nonendocrine diseases. Depending on the clinical presentations and genetic mutation, MEN is divided into different types. The most common syndrome is MEN type 1 (MEN1), characterized by primary hyperparathyroidism secondary to parathyroid gland hyperplasia, pituitary adenoma, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. [1]
Document [2] (Title: Glucagonoma -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies) MEN1 syndrome panel : Obtaining serum parathyroid hormone, gastrin, insulin, pancreatic polypeptide, serotonin, vasoactive intestinal polypeptide (VIP), prolactin, and ACTH levels is essential, as glucagonoma can rarely be associated with the MEN1 syndrome.
Document [3] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Continuing Education Activity) Multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN) constitutes a group of autosomal dominant disorders characterized by a broad spectrum of endocrine and nonendocrine diseases. MEN is divided into different types depending on the clinical presentations and genetic mutation. MEN type 1 (MEN1) is the most common MEN syndrome. MEN type 4 (MEN4) is a rare autosomal dominant endocrine tumor syndrome. The most common tumors seen in this condition involve the parathyroid glands, anterior pituitary, and gastro-entero-pancreatic neuroendocrine tissues. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of MEN4 and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in improving outcomes for patients with this condition.
Document [4] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Complications) Regarding pathophysiology, pituitary tumors can cause pituitary insufficiency and visual field defects. Parathyroid tumors can cause metabolic bone disease and kidney stones. Tumors of gastro-entero-pancreatic tissue can cause excessive secretion of gastrin, insulin, glucagon, or vasoactive intestinal peptide, damaging the gastrointestinal system. Similarly, full-body complications may occur as a result of metastases.
Document [5] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Epidemiology) MEN1 Syndrome is rare, with an incidence of 0.25% and an estimated prevalence between 0.02 to 0.2 per thousand. MEN1 has been shown to affect both males and females equally, which was demonstrated in the study by Gaudet et al., in which of the total 734 MEN1 patients, there was a slight female preponderance (57.8%). The prevalence of pancreatic tumors was higher in men, with the most common manifestation being gastrinoma. The prevalence of pituitary tumors was slightly higher in women, and thymic tumors were exclusively found in men. [4]
Document [6] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Treatment / Management -- Pituitary Tumors) The prevalence of anterior pituitary tumors in MEN1 syndrome is approximately 20% to 60%, and the anterior pituitary tumor can be an initial presentation in about 25% of MEN1 patients. Anterior pituitary tumors in MEN1 are more common in women and are predominantly macroadenomas. [18] Medical management includes the use of cabergoline or bromocriptine in patients with prolactinoma. Octreotide or lanreotide are used in GH-producing tumors.
Document [7] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Evaluation) Evaluation of Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1) involves a comprehensive laboratory and radiographic workup to help identify individual conditions. The following discussion will focus on the workup of the individual parathyroid, anterior pituitary, and neuroendocrine tissue systems involved.
Document [8] (Title: APUDoma(Archived) -- Evaluation) The diagnosis of NETs depends on a thorough history, clinical examination, hormone levels, imaging, and histological confirmation. Carcinoid syndrome should be diagnosed initially using the measurement of 24-hour urinary excretion of 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), which is the metabolite of serotonin, or the plasma level of 5-HIAA, [14] with a sensitivity of 73% and a specificity of 100% in predicting the presence of a midgut NET. [15] Whereas patients who present with suspected pancreatic NET (pNET) should undergo measurement of the corresponding hormone levels, for example, measurement of fasting gastrin blood level for gastrinoma. Additionally, patients with pNETs should be evaluated for MEN type 1 tumors of the pituitary and parathyroid glands.
Document [9] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Prognosis) The prognosis of the MEN1 patient is variable. Tumors associated with MEN1 are more aggressive and challenging to treat. Average life expectancy is shorter in patients with non-functioning pancreatic NET than in patients without pancreatic NET tumors due to tumor expansion complications. Additionally, neuroendocrine tumors can metastasize quickly and can cause premature death. Studies have shown an increased risk of premature death and decreased average life expectancy in MEN1 patients. [23] These patients can also develop significant morbidity from surgical complications; early diagnosis and treatment are of utmost importance.
Document [10] (Title: Pituitary Cancer -- Etiology) Some hereditary conditions are also related to an increased risk of pituitary tumors. For example, multiple endocrine neoplasias (MEN)-1 syndrome is often associated with pituitary adenomas. No environmental or lifestyle changes have been directly linked to the underlying etiology of pituitary tumors, and therefore there are no preventable recommendations at this time. [10] [11]
Document [11] (Title: Pituitary Cancer -- Continuing Education Activity) Pituitary gland tumors arising from the anterior pituitary gland are usually benign. Rarely, pituitary tumors may metastasize and are then termed pituitary carcinoma. Pituitary cancer includes pituitary carcinoma and systemic metastases to the pituitary gland. Some hereditary conditions are also related to an increased risk of pituitary tumors. For example, multiple endocrine neoplasias (MEN)-1 syndrome is often associated with pituitary adenomas. No environmental or lifestyle changes have been directly linked to the underlying etiology of pituitary tumors, and therefore there are no preventable recommendations at this time. This activity presents an overview of pituitary cancer, including its epidemiology, clinical presentation, evaluation, available predictive markers, current and emerging treatment options as well as prognosis.
Document [12] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Etiology) In 2008, MENX was renamed MEN4 at the 11th International Workshop on MENs in Delphi, Greece. [8] Frederiksen et al reviewed 29 cases published before June 2020 and suggested 16 mutations involved in MEN4 (12 missense, three small deletions, and one duplication). [3] This suggests that MEN4 has an autosomal dominant inheritance in humans. The most common presentation is primary hyperparathyroidism due to parathyroid tumors, followed by pituitary tumors (functional and non-functional) and gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors.
Document [13] (Title: APUDoma(Archived) -- History and Physical) NETs mostly arise sporadically, but they also may arise as part of inherited familial syndromes such as multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN-1), which include neoplasia of the anterior pituitary, parathyroid glands, and pancreas. NETs might be symptomatic (functioning) or silent (non-functioning) depending on hormonal secretion. NETs can originate from different organs and from different cell types. Gastroenteropancreatic NETs (GEP-NETs) are subdivided into carcinoid tumors of the luminal GI tract and pancreatic NETs.
Document [14] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Treatment / Management -- Non-Functioning Gastro-Entero-Pancreatic Tumors) Non-functioning pancreatic NETs are the most common gastro-entero-pancreatic tumors. [11] These lesions cause no clinical syndrome and may secrete a small amount of pancreatic polypeptide and gastrin. These represent the most frequent cause of death in MEN1 patients. There can be a delay in diagnosing non-functioning pancreatic NET due to the lack of specific symptoms or clinical findings. Radiological screening for diagnosis of pancreatic NET in MEN1 is recommended at the age of 10 years.
Document [15] (Title: Pituitary Adenoma -- Etiology) The pathogenesis of pituitary adenoma remains unknown. Most of the pituitary adenomas are sporadic. In a study from Iceland with 410 pituitary adenomas, 43% were non-functioning adenomas, 40% prolactin-secreting adenomas, 11 % growth hormone (GH) secreting adenomas, and 6% Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) secreting adenomas. Genetic mutation is rarely a feature of pituitary adenoma.[ 10 ] Familial cases of pituitary adenomas represent 5% of all pituitary tumors. [4] Mutation in the following genes has been found to play a role in the development of pituitary adenomas. Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1): MEN1 is a tumor suppressor gene. Loss of function mutation in this gene leads to tumor formation in the parathyroid, pancreatic, and pituitary glands. [5]
Document [16] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Treatment / Management -- Pituitary Tumors) Studies have shown that anterior pituitary tumors in MEN1 patients are more aggressive and less likely to respond to medical management; thus, they may require surgical management more often. Radiation treatment is used for patients with residual unresectable tumors. Surgical management is performed in patients with large pituitary tumors with compression of the optic nerve, tumors more than 1 cm, or tumors which do not improve with medical management.
Document [17] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Evaluation) MEN4 is phenotypically very similar to MEN1; therefore, the evaluation (laboratory and radiological) of MEN4 to screen for associated clinical conditions is similar to MEN1. Investigations evaluate parathyroid, pituitary, neuroendocrine, and reproductive organ tumors. Carcinoid tumors and meningioma can also occur in patients with MEN4. Specifically, evaluations should focus on the following:
Document [18] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Treatment / Management -- Pancreas) The management approach for nonfunctional or functional neuroendocrine gastropancreatic tumors is similar to that of MEN1. These tumors can cause peptic ulceration due to excess secretion of gastric acid. The clinical syndrome is known as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Surgical resection may be curative if the tumor is fully excised and nonmetastatic. Medical treatment includes proton pump inhibitors and somatostatin analogs. There are no reported cases of insulinoma, VIPoma, glucagonoma, or ectopic ACTH-secreting neuroendocrine tumors in MEN4. [3]
Document [19] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Treatment / Management -- Functioning Gastro-Entero-Pancreatic Tumors) Pancreaticoduodenal NET in MEN1 presents early in life, it is almost always present in multiple, and it has an uncertain behavior with a risk of malignancy. Somatostatin analogs have been used with some success in any stage of the disease and can be a reasonable choice in treating any neuroendocrine tumor. [13]
Document [20] (Title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Etiology -- Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumor Risk Factors) Approximately 10% of pNETs are associated with hereditary syndromes, most notably MEN1, which is caused by a mutation in the MEN1 gene that encodes the tumor suppressor protein menin. Individuals with MEN1 have an increased risk of developing tumors in the parathyroid glands, pituitary gland, and pancreatic islet cells. Other genetic conditions linked to pNETs include von Hippel-Lindau disease, neurofibromatosis type 1, and tuberous sclerosis complex. [1]
Document [21] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Evaluation -- Pancreas) Only a few cases of gastrointestinal and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors are reported in MEN4. Gastrinomas are the most common functional tumors in MEN4. Screening for gastrointestinal and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors should be initiated as per MEN1. [11] [16]
Document [22] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Treatment / Management -- Parathyroid Gland) Currently, there is no specific guidance with regard to the treatment of primary hyperparathyroidism in MEN4. The indication for surgery (parathyroidectomy) remains similar to the MEN1 guidelines. [4] [11] It is important to individualize surgical treatment. Furthermore, surgery should be carried out by an experienced endocrine surgeon.
Document [23] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasias Type 4 -- Pathophysiology -- Pancreas) The prevalence of gastrinomas and nonfunctional pancreatic tumors in MEN4 is approximately 25%. This is much lower than in MEN1. No case reports of insulinoma, vasoactive intestinal peptide-secreting tumor (VIPoma), glucagonoma, or ectopic adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)–secreting neuroendocrine tumors exist in MEN4 cases. [4]
Document [24] (Title: Pituitary Adenoma -- Etiology) Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 4 (MEN4): MEN 4 has a mutation in the cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor 1 B gene (CDKN1B) presenting with pituitary tumors, hyperparathyroidism, testicular, and cervical neuroendocrine tumors. [6]
Document [25] (Title: Hyperpituitarism -- Differential Diagnosis -- Elevated Growth Hormone) Somatotropinomas GHRH-secreting tumors Bronchial carcinomas Carcinoids MEN type-1 Tuberous sclerosis
Document [26] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Pearls and Other Issues) Because of the rarity of the MEN1 syndrome and the complexity of the disease, clinical management is difficult. Most recommendations regarding the management of MEN1 patients lack a high level of evidence suggesting the need for more research in this field. Al-Salameh et al. reported that only 11 out of the 47 recommendations for MEN1 are considered to have a high level of evidence. [1] [24]
Document [27] (Title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Introduction) Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors (pNETs) arise from endocrine islet cells of the pancreas and occur sporadically or in the context of hereditary syndromes such as multiple endocrine neoplasia Type 1 (MEN1), von Hippel-Lindau disease, neurofibromatosis type 1, or tuberous sclerosis complex. They account for only 1% to 2% of pancreatic malignancies, although their incidence has increased over recent decades. These tumors are classified as functional when they secrete bioactive hormones (for example, insulin in insulinoma) or nonfunctional when hormone secretion is absent.
Document [28] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Introduction) MEN1 gene testing in an index case can confirm the diagnosis and allow early detection of asymptomatic mutation carriers years before a MEN1-associated tumor can be detected. However, approximately 20% of MEN1 kindred have no mutation in the MEN1 gene. Unfortunately, there is no genotypic-phenotypic correlation in MEN1, leading to different manifestations even among family members. Studies have demonstrated that nonsense and splicing mutations are associated with more aggressive neuroendocrine tumors. [2]
Document [29] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Treatment / Management -- Carcinoid) Carcinoid tumors affect 3% of MEN1 patients. Common sites of involvement are the gastrointestinal (GI) tract, bronchi, pancreas, and thymus. Somatostatin analogs such as octreotide or lanreotide have been shown to cause regression of gastric carcinoids. [19]
Document [30] (Title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumors -- Epidemiology) The incidence of pNETs is slightly higher in men than in women. They are typically diagnosed in the fifth to seventh decades of life, with a median age at diagnosis of 55 to 60 years. However, patients with hereditary syndromes, eg, MEN1, may present at a younger age. [8] [9] [10]
Document [31] (Title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1 -- Treatment / Management -- Non-Functioning Gastro-Entero-Pancreatic Tumors) Treatment of a non-functioning pancreatic NET is challenging and controversial because of the lack of consensus on surgical indications. Triponez et al. reported that tumor size in non-functioning pancreatic NET correlates with the risk of metastasis and death [11] , but other studies did not confirm similar findings. Clinical practice guidelines for MEN1 recommend surgery if the tumor size is greater than 1 cm, while other studies do not recommend surgery until the size is 20 mm or larger. Surgery can be considered for tumors less than 1 cm if there is rapid growth. So far, tumor size has remained the deciding factor; however, it must be noted that aggressive surgical management can cause significant complications like diabetes mellitus, steatorrhea, and early dumping syndrome.

Document [16] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841) DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a classification that encompasses several distinct disorders of pancreatic endocrine function. It includes مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which denotes diabetes mellitus without any associated complications and represents the basic metabolic disturbance of chronic hyperglycemia. It also comprises اعتلال عصبي سكري, a chronic diabetic neuropathy in which prolonged hyperglycemia damages peripheral nerves, leading to sensory loss, pain, and autonomic dysfunction. The category further lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي, a progressive atrophy of one side of the facial tissues that can be observed in some patients with endocrine pancreatic disease. فرط أنسولين الدم is another child condition, describing an excess of circulating insulin that may arise from insulin‑producing tumors (insulinoma) or other dysregulation of pancreatic β‑cell activity. Finally, ورم غلوكاغوني refers to a glucagon‑secreting pancreatic tumor (glucagonoma) that causes hyperglycemia and a characteristic clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the full spectrum of pancreatic endocrine disorders—from uncomplicated diabetes to its neuropathic complications, hormonal excess states, and neoplastic growths—underscoring the need for comprehensive evaluation and targeted management in patient care.

Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1756) The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” encompasses a spectrum of disorders and clinical manifestations that arise from dysfunction of the pancreatic endocrine system. It includes “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia), which represents elevated blood‑glucose levels and is a primary feature of diabetes. “غيبوبة فرط الأسمولية السكري” (diabetic hyperosmolar coma) is a severe, acute complication of uncontrolled hyperglycemia characterized by extreme dehydration and altered mental status. “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes mellitus without complications) denotes diabetes in its uncomplicated form, serving as the baseline condition from which other sequelae may develop. Among the chronic complications, “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy) reflects nerve damage caused by prolonged hyperglycemia, leading to sensory and motor deficits. “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy) is listed as a possible extra‑pancreatic manifestation that can be associated with underlying endocrine pancreatic disease. Metabolic dysregulation is further illustrated by “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), an excess of circulating insulin that may precede or accompany insulin resistance. Finally, “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) represents a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, producing a distinct clinical syndrome. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse acute, chronic, and neoplastic conditions linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both glycemic control and associated complications.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1303) PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES is defined as an overarching class of endocrine agents that includes both anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues as well as hypothalamic hormones (hypotalamické hormony). In this hierarchy, anterior pituitary lobe hormones and analogues are considered a subset of the broader PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES group, reflecting the close functional relationship between pituitary secretions and their regulatory hypothalamic counterparts. At the same time, the entire PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES category is regarded as a type of hypothalamic hormone group, underscoring the integrated nature of pituitary‑hypothalamic endocrine pathways and the inclusion of synthetic analogues that mimic these natural hormones. This comprehensive classification aids in understanding the shared physiology, therapeutic targeting, and clinical management of disorders involving pituitary and hypothalamic hormone systems.

Together these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations and complications that arise from diseases of the endocrine pancreas, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management in affected patients.

Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 194) The classification “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” is situated within the broader category of “other endocrine disorders.” Within this group, several specific conditions are identified as subtypes or manifestations: “ارتفاع السكر في الدم” (hyperglycemia) represents elevated blood glucose levels; “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” denotes diabetes mellitus without complications; “نقص تركيز السكر” (hypoglycemia) reflects abnormally low blood glucose and is also recognized as a component of the wider “other endocrine disorders” category; “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia) describes excessive circulating insulin; “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma) is a pancreatic tumor that secretes glucagon; and “اضطراب إفراز الجسترين” (gastrin secretion disorder) indicates abnormal gastrin release. The gastrin secretion disorder further leads to “الحثل وإضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ” (gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities), illustrating a downstream effect on the gastric mucosa. Together, these relationships delineate how diabetes‑related endocrine pancreatic diseases encompass a spectrum of hyper‑ and hypoglycemic states, hormone‑excess conditions, neoplastic processes, and secondary gastrointestinal disturbances, all nested within the larger framework of endocrine disorders.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1970) The category “DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES” is positioned within the broader group of “other endocrine disorders” and encompasses a range of specific pancreatic and metabolic conditions. Among its sub‑entities are “مرض السكري دون مضاعفات” (diabetes without complications), “نقص تركيز السكر” (hypoglycemia), “اعتلال عصبي سكري” (diabetic neuropathy), “ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي” (progressive hemifacial atrophy), “فرط أنسولين الدم” (hyperinsulinemia), and “ورم غلوكاغوني” (glucagonoma). Hypoglycemia is therefore recognized both as a distinct manifestation under the diabetes‑related pancreatic disease spectrum and as a condition that belongs to the larger classification of other endocrine disorders. Collectively, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical presentations that can arise from endocrine dysfunction of the pancreas, highlighting the importance of recognizing both isolated metabolic disturbances and their broader endocrine context for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 673) Somatorelin and Metyrapon are both classified as pituitary function test diagnostic agents. As members of this group, they are used to evaluate the activity of the pituitary gland, helping clinicians assess hormone secretion and diagnose endocrine disorders that involve pituitary dysfunction. Their inclusion in the category of pituitary function test diagnostic agents highlights their specific role in testing and monitoring pituitary performance in patient care.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 53) Teriparatid is classified as a member of the parathyroid hormones and analogues group, indicating that it functions as an analogue of parathyroid hormone within this therapeutic class.
